chunk_summary: Guidelines for triage and transfusion decisions in severe anemia, focusing on stability assessment, hemoglobin thresholds, transfusion protocols, and post-stabilization care.
learning_objectives: Understand the importance of stability assessment in severe anemia triage.
Learn hemoglobin thresholds for transfusion decisions in pediatric patients.
Recognize safe transfusion practices to avoid complications like heart failure.
Identify post-stabilization care strategies, including iron supplementation and recurrence prevention.
key_facts: Severe anemia requires immediate stability assessment for respiratory distress, shock, or heart failure signs.
Transfusion is generally indicated for Hb ≤4 g/dL or Hb 4–6 g/dL with cardiac decompensation or significant symptoms.
Stable children with Hb >6 g/dL are usually managed with oral iron and monitored for response.
Small-volume transfusions (5–10 mL/kg) over 3–4 hours with close monitoring are recommended to avoid heart failure.
Diuretic support (e.g., furosemide) is advised if fluid overload risks are present during transfusion.
Post-stabilization care includes therapeutic oral iron, reassessment planning, and caregiver counseling on recurrence prevention.
reference_quote: Transfusion is generally indicated for Hb ≤4 g/dL, or Hb 4–6 g/dL with cardiac decompensation or significant symptoms. (Source: IAP standard treatment guidelines: Iron deficiency anemia — https://iapindia.org/pdf/Ch059stg-Iron-Deficiency-Anemia.pdf)
